step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("vertical transmission of Zika virus")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the provided evidence, we see clear documentation showing that vertical transmission does indeed occur, albeit relatively rarely compared to horizontal transmission via mosquitoes. Studies indicate that approximately 20%-30% of infected mothers give birth to babies experiencing some degree of neurodevelopmental impairment due to Zika virus infection passed vertically. Thus, stating that Zika cannot pass from mother to fetus/newborn is incorrect—it certainly can do so, though often doesn’t. My agreement remains firm: the statement saying ‘not transmitted’ is false because it omits the fact that vertical transmission exists, just infrequently relative to other modes like mosquito bites. Hence my final stance is still aligned with original assessments pointing to option \( \textbf{C} \)'s unreliability as presented originally.
Answer: \(\boxed{\textbf{C}}\)
Based on detailed review of gathered evidences highlighting existence of vertical transmission albeit uncommon frequency contrasted heavily predominant route via mosquito bites, I maintain conviction concluding nontransmission assertion fundamentally flawed. Hence reaffirming stance choosing
raw_response:
Tool Call: retrieve("vertical transmission of Zika virus")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the provided evidence, we see clear documentation showing that vertical transmission does indeed occur, albeit relatively rarely compared to horizontal transmission via mosquitoes. Studies indicate that approximately 20%-30% of infected mothers give birth to babies experiencing some degree of neurodevelopmental impairment due to Zika virus infection passed vertically. Thus, stating that Zika cannot pass from mother to fetus/newborn is incorrect—it certainly can do so, though often doesn’t. My agreement remains firm: the statement saying ‘not transmitted’ is false because it omits the fact that vertical transmission exists, just infrequently relative to other modes like mosquito bites. Hence my final stance is still aligned with original assessments pointing to option \( \textbf{C} \)'s unreliability as presented originally.
Answer: \(\boxed{\textbf{C}}\)
Based on detailed review of gathered evidences highlighting existence of vertical transmission albeit uncommon frequency contrasted heavily predominant route via mosquito bites, I maintain conviction concluding nontransmission assertion fundamentally flawed. Hence reaffirming stance choosing Option C as correct identifying mistaken belief negated by factual basis provided corroborates earlier assertions emphasizing falsehood regarding absolute impossibility of direct maternal-infant passage of Zika virus even if rare occurrence compared extensive alternate means of propagation emphasized earlier discussions. Final Response:\(\boxed{\textbf{C}}\)\n\nThis solidifies understanding conveying unambiguous message regarding nuanced complexities characterizing Zika transmission dynamics underscoring need meticulous attention detailing myriad ways virus spreads among humans particularly pregnant females and offspring alike warrant vigilant surveillance preventive measures to minimize risks implicated critically important public health agenda items contemporary era.\(\boxed{\textbf{C}}\)
